name: Synthetic Fibres and Plastics
subject: chemistry
summary: Man-made polymers — nylon, polyester, acrylic. Plastics are mouldable polymers. Learn polymerisation and why thermoplastics differ from thermosetting plastics.

crushed.title: Polymer = many monomers linked
crushed.text: Poly = many, mer = unit. Thousands of small monomer units join into long chains.

crushed.title: Synthetic fibres
crushed.text: Nylon (strong, elastic), Polyester (Terylene — wrinkle-free), Acrylic (Orlon — wool-like).

crushed.title: Plastics = synthetic polymers
crushed.text: Can be moulded into any shape. Two types: thermoplastics (remouldable) & thermosetting (permanent shape).

crushed.title: Plastics persist
crushed.text: Non-biodegradable — take 100s of years to decompose. Reduce, reuse, recycle.

crushed.title: Teflon is special
crushed.text: PTFE coating — non-stick, heat resistant. Used on pans. Monomer: tetrafluoroethene.

items.label: Nylon
items.detail: First fully synthetic fibre. Strong, elastic. Ropes, parachutes, socks

items.label: Polyester (Terylene)
items.detail: Wrinkle-free, quick-drying. Blended with cotton for terrycot

items.label: Acrylic (Orlon)
items.detail: Wool-like warmth. Sweaters, blankets — cheaper than wool

items.label: Rayon
items.detail: Semi-synthetic — from wood pulp. Feels like silk

items.label: Thermoplastics
items.detail: Soften on heating, harden on cooling. Remouldable — e.g. PVC, polythene

items.label: Thermosetting
items.detail: Permanent shape once set. Cannot remould — e.g. bakelite, melamine

demos.title: Polymerisation — Making Polythene
demos.description: Many ethene monomers join head-to-tail to form long polythene chains.

steps.label: Chains form
steps.description: Thousands of ethene units link into long polymer chains

steps.label: Solid polythene
steps.description: White waxy solid — bags, bottles, pipes made from this

demos.title: Nylon Formation (Condensation)
demos.description: Two different monomers join with loss of a small molecule (H₂O) — condensation polymerisation.

steps.label: Condensation
steps.description: Monomers link; H₂O molecule released with each bond

steps.label: Long nylon chain
steps.description: Strong, elastic nylon polymer forms at interface

steps.label: Famous demo
steps.description: Nylon rope can be pulled from between two solutions!

flows.title: Life Cycle of a Plastic Bottle
flows.description: From petroleum to bottle to recycling (or landfill).

steps.label: Petroleum
steps.description: Petroleum fractionated to get ethene monomer.

steps.label: Moulding
steps.description: Heated and blow-moulded into bottle shape.

steps.label: Use & discard
steps.description: Bottle used once — then trash or recycling bin.

steps.label: Recycle
steps.description: Thermoplastics can be melted and reshaped — reducing waste.

keyTerms.definition: Small repeating unit that joins to form a polymer
Large molecule made of many repeating monomer units
keyTerms.analogy: A long chain of connected LEGO bricks

keyTerms.definition: Chemical process joining monomers into polymers
keyTerms.analogy: Clicking LEGO bricks together into a chain

keyTerms.definition: Plastic that softens on heating and can be remoulded

chapterSummary.overview: Synthetic fibres (nylon, polyester, acrylic, rayon) and plastics are man-made polymers. This chapter covers how they are made, their properties, advantages over natural fibres, types of plastics (thermoplastics vs thermosetting), and environmental concerns including biodegradability.
keyPoints: Synthetic fibres are made from petrochemicals through polymerisation.
Nylon: strong, elastic — ropes, parachutes; Polyester: wrinkle-free fabrics.
Rayon (artificial silk) from wood pulp; Acrylic resembles wool.
Thermoplastics can be remoulded (polythene, PVC); thermosetting cannot (bakelite).
Plastics are non-biodegradable — cause pollution; the 4 R's: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover.
chapterSummary.examFocus: Compare natural vs synthetic fibres. Differentiate thermoplastics vs thermosetting. Discuss plastic disposal problems.
